title: 第 101 節
en: 15. Next follows the father's tradition to the son, and thus they explain it K The father, when going to depart, calls his son, after having strewn the house with fresh grass, and having laid the sacrificial fire, and having placed near it a pot of water with a jug (full of rice), himself covered with a new cloth, and dressed in white. He places himself above his son, touching his organs with his own organs, or he may deliver the tradition to him while he sits before him. Then he delivers it to him. The father says : ' Let me place my speech in thee.' The son says : ' I take thy speech in me.' The father says : ' Let me place my scent (pra^a) in thee.' The son says : ' I take thy scent in me.' The father says : ' Let me place my eye in thee.' The son says : ' I take thy eye in me.' The father says : ' Let me place my ear in thee.' The son says : ' I take thy ear in me.' The father says : ' Let me place my tastes of food in thee.' The son says : ' I take thy tastes of food in me.' The father says : ' Let me place my actions 1 Cf. Br*had-ara«yaka I, 5, 17. U 2 292 KAUSHITAKI-UPANISHAD.

in thee.' The son says : ' I take thy actions in me.' The father says : ' Let me place my pleasure and pain in thee.' The son says : ' I take thy pleasure and pain in me.' The father says : ' Let me place happiness, joy, and offspring in thee.' The son says : ' I take thy happiness, joy, and offspring in me.' The father says : ' Let me place my walking in thee.' The son says : ' I take thy walking in me V The father says : ' Let me place my mind in thee.' The son says : ' I take thy mind in me.' The father says : ' Let me place my knowledge (pra^na) in thee.' The son says : ' I take thy knowledge in me.' But if the father is very ill, he may say shortly : ' Let me place my spirits (pra?2as) in thee,' and the son : ' I take thy spirits in me.'

Then the son walks round his father keeping his right side towards him, and goes away. The father calls after him : ' May fame, glory of countenance, and honour always follow thee.' Then the other looks back over his left shoulder, covering himself with his hand or the hem of his garment, saying : ' Obtain the heavenly worlds (svarga) and all desires.'

If the father recovers, let him be under the authority of his son, or let him wander about (as an ascetic). But if he departs, then let them despatch him, as he ought to be despatched, yea, as he ought to be despatched2.
1 Another jakha adds here dhiyah, the thoughts (active), vig-ilatavyam, their object, and kama^, desires.

2 I have taken samapayati in the sense of performing the last duties towards a dead person, though I confess I know of no parallel passage in which samapayati occurs in that sense. Professor Cowell translates : ' If he dies, then let them cause the son duly to receive the tradition, as the tradition is to be given.' The text itself varies, for the reading presupposed by the commentator is enaw (putram) samapayati, instead of ena;tf samapayeyu/i.
Ill ADHYAYA, I. 293 Third Adhyaya1.

1. Pratardana, forsooth, the son of Divodasa (king of Ka.fi), came by means of fighting and strength to the beloved abode of Indra. Indra said to him : ' Pratardana, let me give you a boon to choose.' And Pratardana answered : ' Do you yourself choose that boon for me which you deem most beneficial for a man.' Indra said to him : ' No one who chooses, chooses for another ; choose thyself.' Then Pratardana replied : ' Then that boon to choose is no boon for me.'

Then, however, Indra did not swerve from the truth, for Indra is truth. Indra said to him: 'Know me only; that is what I deem most beneficial for man, that he should know me. I slew the three-headed son of Tvashz'rz; I delivered the Arunmukhas, the devotees, to the wolves (salavrzka) ; breaking many treaties, I killed the people of Prahlada in heaven, the people of Puloma in the sky, the people of Kalakanfa on earth2. And not one hair of me was harmed there. And he who knows me thus, by no deed of his is his life harmed, not by the murder of 1 The object now is to explain the true Brahma-vidya, while the first and second chapters are only introductory, treating of the worship of the couch (paryahkopasana) and of the worship of prawa.

2 This refers to heroic deeds performed by Indra, as represented in the hymns of the Rig-veda. See Rig-veda V, 34, 4, and Sayawa's commentary; Ait. Brahm. VII, 28. Weber, Indische Studien I, 410-418, has tried to discover an original physical meaning in the heroic deeds ascribed to Indra. A curious remark is made by the commentator, who says that the skulls of the Arunmukhas were turned into the thorns of the desert (karira) which remain to this day, — a very common phase in popular tradition.
294 kaushitaki-upanishad.
his mother, not by the murder of his father, not by theft, not by the killing of a Brahman. If he is going to commit a sin, the bloom ] does not depart from his face.'

2. Indra said : ' I am pra^a, meditate on me as the conscious self (pra^natman), as life, as immortality. Life is pra?za, pra^a is life. Immortality is pra/za, pra^a is immortality. As long as pra;za dwells in this body, so long surely there is life. By pra^a he obtains immortality in the other world, by knowledge true conception. He who meditates on me as life and immortality, gains his full life in this world, and obtains in the Svarga world immortality and indestructibility.'

(Pratardana said) : 'Some maintain here, that the pra/zas become one, for (otherwise) no one could at the same time make known a name by speech, see a form with the eye, hear a sound with the ear, think a thought with the mind. After having become one, the prawas perceive all these together, one by one. While speech speaks, all pra^as speak after it. While the eye sees, all pra^as see after it. While the ear hears, all pra/zas hear after it. While the mind thinks, all pra?zas think after it. While the pra;za breathes, all pra;zas breathe after it.'
' Thus it is indeed,' said Indra, ' but nevertheless there is a pre-eminence among the pra;zas 2.
3. Man lives deprived of speech, for we see dumb people. Man lives deprived of sight, for we see 1 Professor Cowell compares Taittiriya-Sa^hita III, 1,1, nasya mtam na haro vyeti.
2 Pra/zas, in the plural, is supposed to stand for the five senses as modifications of breath. It would be better if we could read prawasya ni/foreyasam. See before, II, 14.

Ill ADHYAYA, 3. 295 blind people. Man lives deprived of hearing, for we see deaf people. Man lives deprived of mind, for we see infants. Man lives deprived of his arms, deprived of his legs, for we see it thus. But pra^a alone is the conscious self (pra^natman), and having laid hold of this body, it makes it rise up. Therefore it is said, Let man worship it alone as uktha 1. What is prana, that is pragma (self-consciousness) ; what is pra^fia (self-consciousness), that is pra;^a, for together they (pra^na and pra^a) live in this body, and together they go out of it. Of that, this is the evidence, this is the understanding. When a man, being thus asleep, sees no dream whatever, he becomes one with that pra^a alone 2. Then speech goes to him (when he is absorbed in pra.ua) with all names, the eye with all forms, the ear with all sounds, the mind with all thoughts. And when he awakes, then, as from a burning fire sparks proceed in all directions, thus from that self the pra^as (speech, &c.) proceed, each towards its place ; from the pra^as the gods (Agni, &c), from the gods the worlds.

Of this, this is the proof, this is the understanding. When a man is thus sick, going to die, falling into weakness and faintness, they say: ' His thought has departed, he hears not, he sees not, he speaks not, he thinks not.' Then he becomes one with that pra/^a alone. Then speech goes to him (who is absorbed in pra^a) with all names, the eye with all 1 Uktha, hymn, is artificially derived from ut-thapayati, to raise up, and hence uktha, hymn, is to be meditated on as prawa, breath, which likewise raises up the body. See Ait. Ar. II, 1, 15.
2 He is absorbed in prawa. Or should it be pra/za^ as nominative ?
forms, the ear with all sounds, the mind with all thoughts. And when he departs from this body, he departs together with all these 1.

4. Speech gives up to him (who is absorbed in pra^a) all names, so that by speech he obtains all names. The nose gives up to him all odours, so that by scent he obtains all odours. The eye gives up to him all forms, so that by the eye he obtains all forms. The ear gives up to him all sounds, so that by the ear he obtains all sounds. The mind gives up to him all thoughts, so that by the mind he obtains all thoughts. This is the complete absorption in pra/za. And what is pra^a is pra^fia (selfconsciousness), what is pra^na (self-consciousness) is pra^a. For together do these two live in the body, and together do they depart.
Now we shall explain how all things become one in that pra^fia (self-consciousness).

5. Speech is one portion taken out2 of pra^fia (self-conscious knowledge), the word is its object, placed outside. The nose is one portion taken out of it, the odour is its object, placed outside. The eye is one portion taken out of it, the form is its object, placed outside. The ear is one portion taken out of it, the sound is its object, placed outside. The tongue is one portion taken out of it, the taste of food is its object, placed outside. The two hands 1 According to another reading we might translate, ' Speech takes away all names from that body; and pra«a, in which speech is absorbed, thus obtains all names.'

2 I read udu//;am or udiV/fom, instead of adud/iam, explained by the commentator as aduduhat. Professor Cowell translates, ' Speech verily milked one portion thereof,' which may have been the original purport of the writer.

Ill ADHYAYA, J. 29 J are one portion taken out of it, their action is their object, placed outside. The body is one portion taken out of it, its pleasure and pain are its object, placed outside. The organ is one portion taken out of it, happiness, joy, and offspring are its object, placed outside. The two feet are one portion taken out of it, movements are their object, placed outside. Mind is one portion taken out of it, thoughts and desires are its object, placed outside.

6. Having by pragma (self-conscious knowledge) taken possession of speech, he obtains by speech all words. Having by prat^fia taken possession of the nose, he obtains all odours. Having by pra^fia taken possession of the eye, he obtains all forms. Having by pra^na taken possession of the ear, he obtains all sounds. Having by pra^na taken possession of the tongue, he obtains all tastes of food. Having by pra^na taken possession of the two hands, he obtains all actions. Having by pra^na taken possession of the body, he obtains pleasure and pain. Having by pragma taken possession of the organ, he obtains happiness, joy, and offspring. Having by pra^fia taken possession of the two feet, he obtains all movements. Having by pra^fla taken possession of mind, he obtains all thoughts.

7. For without pragma (self-consciousness) speech does not make known (to the self) any word *. ' My 1 Professor Cowell has translated a passage from the commentary which is interesting as showing that its author and the author of the Upanishad too had a clear conception of the correlative nature of knowledge. ' The organ of sense,' he says, ' cannot exist without pra^Tia (self-consciousness), nor the objects of sense be obtained without the organ, therefore — on the principle, that when one thing cannot exist without another, that thing is said to be identical with the other — as the cloth, for instance, being 298 KAUSHITAKI-UPANISHAD.

mind was absent,' he says, ' I did not perceive that word.' Without pra^na the nose does not make known any odour. ' My mind was absent,' he says, ' I did not perceive that odour.' Without pra^ fia the eye does not make known any form. ' My mind was absent,' he says, ' I did not perceive that form.' Without pra^fia the ear does not make known any sound. ' My mind was absent,' he says, ' I did not perceive that sound.' Without pra^fia the tongue does not make known any taste. ' My mind was absent,' he says, ' I did not perceive that taste.' Without pra^na the two hands do not make known any act. ' Our mind was absent,' they say, ' we did not perceive any act.' Without pra^na the body does not make known pleasure or pain. ' My mind was absent,' he says, ' I did not perceive that pleasure or pain.' Without pra^-fia the organ does not make known happiness, joy, or offspring. ' My mind was absent,' he says, ' I did not perceive that happiness, joy, or offspring.' Without pra^na the two feet do not make known any movement. ' Our mind was absent,' they say, ' we did not perceive that movement.' Without pra^na no thought succeeds, nothing can be known that is to be known.

8. Let no man try to find out what speech is, let him know the speaker. Let no man try to find out what odour is, let him know him who smells. Let no man try to find out what form is, let him know the seer. Let no man try to find out what sound is, let never perceived without the threads, is identical with them, or the (false perception of) silver being never found without the mother of pearl is identical with it, so the objects of sense being never found without the organs are identical with them, and the organs being never found without pra^iia (self-consciousness) are identical with it.
zh: 15. 接下來是父親向兒子傳授傳統，因此他們如此解釋：父親將要離世時，召喚他的兒子，先是在房屋裡鋪撒新鮮的草，放置祭祀火焰，在火焰旁邊放一個盛滿水的罐子和一個裝米的瓶子，自己用新布披覆，穿著白衣。他在兒子上方放置自己，用自己的器官接觸兒子的器官，或者他可以在兒子坐在他面前時將傳統傳授給他。然後他向他傳授。父親說：「讓我將我的言語置於你之中。」兒子說：「我接納你的言語置於我之中。」父親說：「讓我將我的氣息（prāṇa）置於你之中。」兒子說：「我接納你的氣息置於我之中。」父親說：「讓我將我的眼睛置於你之中。」兒子說：「我接納你的眼睛置於我之中。」父親說：「讓我將我的耳朵置於你之中。」兒子說：「我接納你的耳朵置於我之中。」父親說：「讓我將我的食物滋味置於你之中。」兒子說：「我接納你的食物滋味置於我之中。」父親說：「讓我將我的行為 --- 註1 比較《婆羅多阿蘭亞迦奧義書》I, 5, 17。

在你身上。』兒子說：『我接納你的行為在我身上。』父親說：『讓我將我的快樂與痛苦放在你身上。』兒子說：『我接納你的快樂與痛苦在我身上。』父親說：『讓我將幸福、喜悅與子嗣放在你身上。』兒子說：『我接納你的幸福、喜悅與子嗣在我身上。』父親說：『讓我將我的行走放在你身上。』兒子說：『我接納你的行走在我身上。』父親說：『讓我將我的心念放在你身上。』兒子說：『我接納你的心念在我身上。』父親說：『讓我將我的知識（prá*na*）放在你身上。』兒子說：『我接納你的知識在我身上。』然而，若父親病重，他可簡言之：『讓我將我的靈氣（prá*nas*）放在你身上，』兒子則說：『我接納你的靈氣在我身上。』
隨後兒子繞著他的父親行走，保持右側朝向父親，然後離去。父親在身後喊道：「願名聲、容光煥發的榮耀及尊崇永遠追隨你。」那時另一人從左肩向後看，用手或衣裳下擺遮蔽自己，說道：「獲得天界（svarga）及一切願望。」
若父親康復，則應由其子掌權，或任其出家遁世。然若彼已辭世，則應按其所應之禮予以送終，誠然，應按其所應之禮予以送終²。
另一位雅卡（jakha）在此添加了 dhiyah（諸思想〔主動〕）、vig-ilatavyam（其對象）與 kāma（欲望）。
2 我採納了 samapayati 作為「對死者履行最後義務」的含義，雖然我承認我不知道有任何平行段落中 samapayati 具有該義涵。考威爾教授翻譯為：「若其死亡，則令其子得以應當接受傳統，如傳統之應被傳授者。」經文本身有異文，蓋註釋者所預設的讀法為 enaw (putram) samapayati，而非 ena;tf samapayeyu/i。
**III ADHYAYA, I. 293** 第三部分¹ --- 1. Adhyaya 一詞意為「部分」或「章節」，源自梵文。此處指吠陀或其他經典的第三個主要分部。
普拉塔爾達那乃迪沃達薩（喀西王）之子，藉戰鬥與力量而至因陀羅之心愛居所。因陀羅語之曰：「普拉塔爾達那，吾願賜汝一樁恩德，汝可自擇。」普拉塔爾達那答曰：「願汝自擇於吾之恩德，誠如汝所認為最於人有益者。」因陀羅語之曰：「無有一人為他人而選擇；汝當自擇。」普拉塔爾達那乃答曰：「則此選擇恩德之樁，於吾無益矣。」

然而，因陀羅並未背離真理，因為因陀羅就是真理。因陀羅對他說：「唯當認識我；我認為這對人類最為有益，即他應當認識我。我殺死了特瓦什茲之三頭子嗣；我將阿魯恩穆卡斯（Arunmukhas）這些虔誠者交付給狼群（salavrzka）；我破毀了眾多盟約，殺死了普拉赫拉達（Prahlada）在天界的子民、普羅馬（Puloma）在天空的子民、卡拉堪塔（Kalakanfa）在大地上的子民。而我在那裡連一根毛髮都未曾受損。凡如此認識我者，他的生命不會因任何行為而受損害，不會因殺人而受損害—— 1 其目的現在是闡明真正的梵文知識（Brahma-vidya），而第一章和第二章則僅為導論性質，論述床座的祭祀（paryahkopasana）和普拉那（prawa）的祭祀。

2 這裡指的是因陀羅在梨俱吠陀讚歌中所代表的英雄業跡。參見梨俱吠陀 V, 34, 4 及沙耶瓦（Sayawa）的註疏；愛迪塔耶婆羅門書（Ait. Brahm.）VII, 28。韋伯（Weber）在《印度研究》第 I 卷 410-418 頁中曾試圖在賦予因陀羅的英雄業跡中發現原初的物理意涵。評註者提出了一個有趣的看法，說阿倫穆卡人（Arunmukhas）的頭骨被轉變成了沙漠荊棘（karira），時至今日仍然存在——這是民間傳統中極為常見的說法。
294 考什塔基奧義書（kaushitaki-upanishad）。
他的母親，不是因為殺害他的父親，不是因為盜竊，不是因為殺死婆羅門。如果他將要犯罪，光彩也不會從他的臉上消褪。』

2. 因陀羅說：「我即是生命氣（prana），當冥想我為有意識的自我（pranātman），作為生命，作為不朽者。生命即是生命氣（prana），生命氣即是生命。不朽即是生命氣（prana），生命氣即是不朽。只要生命氣駐留於此身，生命便確實存焉。藉由生命氣，他在彼世得證不朽；藉由知識，得證真實的認識。誰人冥想我為生命與不朽，此人在此世獲得其完整的生命，並在斯瓦爾迦（Svarga）世界中獲得不朽與不可毀滅性。」

（普拉塔爾達納說）：「有人在此主張，諸般生命力（pra?as）成為一體，因為（否則的話）沒有人能夠同時藉由言語說出一個名字、用眼睛看見一個形態、用耳朵聽聞一個聲音、用心靈思考一個思想。在成為一體之後，諸般生命力（prawas）一個接著一個地共同知覺所有這些事物。當言語說話時，所有生命力都在它之後說話。當眼睛看見時，所有生命力都在它之後看見。當耳朵聽聞時，所有生命力都在它之後聽聞。當心靈思考時，所有生命力都在它之後思考。當生命力呼吸時，所有生命力都在它之後呼吸。」
「確實是如此，」因陀羅說，「但儘管如此，在諸 pra;zas 中仍有優越性存在。」
3. 人類生活可以沒有言語，因為我們看到有啞人存在。人類生活可以沒有視覺，因為我們看到¹ 考韋爾教授比較《泰迪利亞奧義書》(Taittiriya-Saṁhita) III, 1,1，nasya mtam na haro vyeti。
2 Pra/zas 的複數形式據說代表作為呼吸變現的五感。若能讀作 prawasya ni/foreyasam 會更好。參閱前文第二卷第 14 節。

盲人。人生活中被剝奪了聽覺，因為我們看到聾子。人生活中被剝奪了心智，因為我們看到嬰兒。人生活中被剝奪了他的雙臂、雙腿，因為我們看到確實如此。但唯有生命氣息（prāṇa）才是有意識的自我（prāṇātman），掌握了這個身體之後，它使身體站起來。因此有言，讓人單獨敬拜它作為讚歌（uktha）¹。生命氣息（prāṇa）是什麼，自我意識（pragña）就是什麼；自我意識（pragña）是什麼，生命氣息（prāṇa）就是什麼，因為它們（prāṇa 和 pragña）一起生活在這個身體裡，一起離開它。對於這一點，這是證據，這是理解。當一個人這樣睡著了，根本看不到任何夢，他就只與那個生命氣息（prāṇa）合為一體²。那時言語來到他那裡（當他被吸收在 prāṇa 中時）帶著所有的名稱，眼睛帶著所有的形相，耳朵帶著所有的聲音，心智帶著所有的思想。當他醒來時，就像從燃燒的火焰中火花向四面八方射出一樣，同樣地從那個自我中，生命氣息們（言語等等）射出，各自向它們的地方；從生命氣息們是提婆（神），從提婆是世界。

對此，這是其證明，這是其理解。當一個人如此患病，走向死亡，陷入虛弱和昏迷時，人們說：「他的思想已經離去，他聽不見，他看不見，他不說話，他不思考。」那時他便與那*pra*ṇa（氣息）單獨合而為一。於是言語帶著一切名稱歸向他（被吸收於*pra*ṇa者），眼睛帶著一切¹ ¹ *Uktha*（讚歌）是從*ut-thapayati*（舉起）人工衍生而來，因此*uktha*（讚歌）應當被冥想為*pra*ṇa（氣息），氣息同樣舉起身體。見《艾氏梵書》（*Ait. Ar.*）II, 1, 15。
他沉浸於 pra**wa**。或者作為主格應該是 pra/**za**^？
形式、耳朵與所有聲音、心智與所有思想。當他離開此身體時，他便與所有這些一同離開 1。

4. 言語將一切名稱交付於他（沉浸於 pra^a 者），如此藉由言語他得獲一切名稱。鼻將一切氣味交付於他，如此藉由嗅覺他得獲一切氣味。眼將一切形色交付於他，如此藉由眼睛他得獲一切形色。耳將一切聲音交付於他，如此藉由耳朵他得獲一切聲音。心將一切思想交付於他，如此藉由心識他得獲一切思想。這就是圓滿的沉浸於 pra/za。而 pra^a 就是 pra^fia（自我意識），pra^na（自我意識）就是 pra^a。蓋此二者共同生活於身體之中，亦共同離去。
現在我們將闡明一切事物如何在那個 prajñā（自我意識）中合而為一。
5. 言語是從智識（prajñā，自覺的知識）中取出的一部分，言詞是它置於外部的客體。鼻是從它中取出的一部分，氣味是它置於外部的客體。眼是從它中取出的一部分，形色是它置於外部的客體。耳是從它中取出的一部分，聲音是它置於外部的客體。舌是從它中取出的一部分，食物的味道是它置於外部的客體。雙手1 根據另一種讀法，我們可能會譯為：「言語奪取那身體的一切名稱；言語被吸收其中的智識，由此獲得一切名稱。」
我讀作 udu//;am 或 udiV/fom，而非 adud/iam，經評註者解釋為 aduduhat。考韋爾教授的譯文為「言語確實擠出其中一部分」(Speech verily milked one portion thereof)，這或許是作者的本意。
III ADHYAYA, J. 29 J 是從中取出的一個部分，它們的作用是它們的對象，置於外部。身體是從中取出的一個部分，它的快樂和痛苦是它的對象，置於外部。器官是從中取出的一個部分，幸福、喜悅和後代是它的對象，置於外部。雙足是從中取出的一個部分，運動是它們的對象，置於外部。心靈是從中取出的一個部分，思想和慾望是它的對象，置於外部。

6. 藉由知識（pragña，自我意識的知識）掌握言語，他藉由言語獲得一切言辭。藉由知識（pragña）掌握鼻子，他獲得一切氣味。藉由知識（pragña）掌握眼睛，他獲得一切形色。藉由知識（pragña）掌握耳朵，他獲得一切聲音。藉由知識（pragña）掌握舌頭，他獲得食物的一切滋味。藉由知識（pragña）掌握雙手，他獲得一切行動。藉由知識（pragña）掌握身體，他獲得快樂與痛苦。藉由知識（pragña）掌握器官，他獲得幸福、歡喜與子嗣。藉由知識（pragña）掌握雙足，他獲得一切運動。藉由知識（pragña）掌握心識，他獲得一切思想。

沒有語用論（prajna，自我意識），言語就不能表達任何詞彙給自我¹。 ¹ 考威爾教授譯過註釋書中的一段文字，這段文字有趣之處在於，它表明其作者及《奧義書》的作者對知識的相關性都有清晰的概念。他說：「感覺器官不能無語用論（prajna，自我意識）而存在，感覺對象亦不能無器官而獲得；因此——基於這個原則：當一物不能無另一物而存在時，那物就被說為與另一物相同——如布匹之例……」298 《考施塔基奧義書》（KAUSHITAKI-UPANISHAD）。

心靈缺席時，他說：『我的心靈不在，我沒有覺知那個詞語。』沒有生命氣（prāṇa），鼻子不能認知任何氣味。『我的心靈不在，』他說，『我沒有覺知那個氣味。』沒有生命氣（prāṇa），眼睛不能認知任何形象。『我的心靈不在，』他說，『我沒有覺知那個形象。』沒有生命氣（prāṇa），耳朵不能認知任何聲音。『我的心靈不在，』他說，『我沒有覺知那個聲音。』沒有生命氣（prāṇa），舌頭不能認知任何滋味。『我的心靈不在，』他說，『我沒有覺知那個滋味。』沒有生命氣（prāṇa），兩隻手不能認知任何行為。『我們的心靈不在，』他們說，『我們沒有覺知任何行為。』沒有生命氣（prāṇa），身體不能認知快樂或痛苦。『我的心靈不在，』他說，『我沒有覺知那個快樂或痛苦。』沒有生命氣（prāṇa），生殖器不能認知幸福、喜悅或後嗣。『我的心靈不在，』他說，『我沒有覺知那個幸福、喜悅或後嗣。』沒有生命氣（prāṇa），兩隻腳不能認知任何運動。『我們的心靈不在，』他們說，『我們沒有覺知那個運動。』沒有生命氣（prāṇa），思想無法成就，任何應被認知之物都無法被認知。

8. 莫使任何人試圖探明何謂言語，而應認識說話者。莫使任何人試圖探明何謂氣味，而應認識嗅覺者。莫使任何人試圖探明何謂形體，而應認識觀看者。莫使任何人試圖探明何謂聲音，而應認識聽聞者。感官對象絕不能離開感官而被知覺，正如線絲（thread）絕不能離開線軸而被知覺一樣；感官對象若非離開感官而獨立存在，則與感官相同；同樣，銀的虛妄認知（false perception of silver）絕不能離開珍珠貝母而被發現，故必與之相同。因此，感官對象既不能離開感官器官而被發現，則與器官相同；而感官器官既不能離開意識（prajñā）而被發現，則與意識相同。
